mnemonics.title: Design Thinking Process
mnemonics.content: Remember as 'E-DI-P-T': Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test. Like a detective (Empathize), solving a mystery (Define), brainstorming solutions (Ideate), building a prototype (Prototype), and testing the solution (Test).

mnemonics.title: Emotional Design Levels
mnemonics.content: Remember as 'V-B-R': Visceral (first impression), Behavioral (during use), Reflective (after use). Like meeting someone: first impression (Visceral), your experience talking with them (Behavioral), what you think about them later (Reflective).

mnemonics.title: User Research Methods
mnemonics.content: Remember 'I-S-C-U': Interviews, Surveys, Card Sorting, Usability Testing. The 'I' starts the research, 'S' collects data, 'C' organizes information, 'U' validates the design.

acronyms.usage: Design approach that prioritizes user needs throughout the design process
Design approach focused on achieving specific user and business objectives
Standards for making web content accessible to people with disabilities
Study of how humans interact with computers and design principles for this interaction
Visual interface that allows users to interact with devices through graphical elements

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a newspaper layout - the biggest headline grabs attention first, then smaller headlines, then body text. Size, color, and position create a clear reading order.
Think of reaching for a large button on a phone (easy, fast) vs. a tiny 'x' to close a popup (hard, slow). Make important targets large and place them where they're easy to reach.
Think of a crowded desk vs. a clean desk. A cluttered interface overwhelm the brain; a clean one lets you focus on what's important.
A door with a push plate affords pushing; one with a handle affords pulling. In UI, buttons that look raised afford clicking.

analogies.analogy: Like a host who pays attention to what guests need - their comfort, preferences, and problems - rather than just arranging furniture according to the host's taste.
Like a restaurant experience: the ambience (visceral), the food quality (behaviorial), and the memory of the birthday dinner (reflective) all matter.
Like a city planning - individual buildings matter, but how they connect (roads, utilities, signs) determines if the city works smoothly.
Like a restaurant menu - you don't see all 50 dishes at once. You see categories, then options within categories, keeping the choice manageable.
